title: As Goes AI Compute, So Goes Ethernet Networking
gist: AI's shift from training to production inference is driving datacenter Ethernet switch sales far above historical trends (datacenter Ethernet up 61% to ~$10B in Q1 2026), with Nvidia's SpectrumX now the #1 datacenter Ethernet switch vendor ahead of Cisco and Arista, and an aggressive transition to 800 Gb/sec ports collapsing cost per bit.

themes.theme: Ethernet Market Outpacing Servers and AI Compute

insights.claim: GenAI broadening from model training to production inference is expanding demand for Ethernet switches, NICs, and DPUs faster than historical networking trends because Ethernet is absorbing InfiniBand-like capabilities.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Position toward Ethernet networking suppliers (switch/NIC/DPU makers) as inference scale-out, not just training, becomes the volume driver of AI datacenter spend.
insights.quote: the broadening out of GenAI from model training to production inference means that bog standard Ethernet, which is evolving to have many of the capabilities of InfiniBand
insights.locator: InfiniBand may still be the preferred interconnect

insights.claim: In Q1 2026 the Ethernet switch market grew faster than the overall server market: servers rose 30.4 percent to $122.62 billion while Ethernet switch sales rose 39.8 percent to $15.36 billion.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Networking is taking a rising share of AI infrastructure wallet, so networking pure-plays can outgrow server OEMs in the buildout.
insights.quote: the overall server market grew by 30.4 percent to $122.62 billion in Q1, while Ethernet switch sales ... rose by 39.8 percent to $15.36 billion
insights.locator: To be specific, the overall server market grew

insights.claim: Datacenter Ethernet switch sales rose 61 percent to just over $10 billion in Q1 2026, versus only 12.3 percent growth to $5.35 billion for edge and campus switches, showing spend is concentrated in bigger, faster AI datacenter networks.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The growth is datacenter-specific; favor vendors with datacenter exposure over those weighted to enterprise edge/campus.
insights.quote: Ethernet in the glass house ... rose by 61 percent to just a hair over $10 billion
insights.locator: If you drill down further, isolating datacenter

insights.claim: A meaningful portion of Ethernet revenue growth reflects switch component price hikes, particularly for components with DRAM memory embedded for auxiliary storage, not purely unit volume.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Some of the revenue surge is price/memory-cost driven and could unwind if DRAM prices ease, so parse switch-vendor revenue growth for pricing versus unit demand.
insights.quote: a fairly good portion of the revenue increases we see in the Ethernet space are due to the hikes in switch component prices, particularly those that have DRAM memory embedded in them
insights.locator: It is hard to say for sure

insights.claim: Router revenues grew 11.3 percent to $3.75 billion in Q1 2026 despite modern switch ASICs from Broadcom, Marvell, and Cisco including routing functions, because many buyers cannot turn switches into routers with their own network operating system.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Standalone router demand persists as an NOS-capability moat, protecting Cisco/Juniper/Huawei router revenue against merchant-silicon commoditization.
insights.quote: modern switch ASICs from Broadcom, Marvell, and Cisco Systems include routing functions as well as those for switching
insights.locator: The router market keeps plugging along

insights.claim: DriveNets sells a router operating system called Network Cloud that runs on commodity hardware to disaggregate the NOS from the switch like a hyperscaler, but even hyperscalers still buy commercial routers from Cisco, Juniper Networks, and Huawei Technologies.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: NOS disaggregation is a real but partial threat; commercial router incumbents retain hyperscaler demand, capping the near-term disruption to Cisco/Juniper.
insights.quote: DriveNets has a router operating system called Network Cloud that can be deployed on commodity hardware
insights.locator: DriveNets has a router operating system

insights.claim: Service providers and cloud SPs drove $2.9 billion of Q1 router revenue, 77.2 percent of the total and up 12.9 percent, while enterprise router sales were only $867 million and grew 6.1 percent as enterprises stretch upgrade cycles.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Router demand is a service-provider/cloud story, not enterprise; weight router-vendor theses toward carrier and cloud capex cycles.
insights.quote: accounted for $2.9 billion in router revenues in Q1, which was 77.2 percent of router sales during the period
insights.locator: In fact, service providers and what IDC calls

insights.claim: Router sales rose 19.6 percent in the Americas versus 9.8 percent in EMEA and just 2.8 percent in Asia/Pacific in Q1 2026, indicating the United States is emphatically winning the IT infrastructure arms race.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: US-centric datacenter buildout is outpacing other regions; tilt AI-infra exposure toward vendors levered to North American hyperscaler capex.
insights.quote: If IT infrastructure is an arms race, the United States is clearly and emphatically winning according to all of the data I have seen in the past several years.
insights.locator: Router sales in the Americas rose by 19.6 percent

themes.theme: Nvidia Overtakes Cisco and Arista in Datacenter Ethernet

insights.claim: Nvidia became the number one supplier of datacenter Ethernet switches, surpassing both Cisco and Arista Networks, with the crossover having occurred back in Q2 2025.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Nvidia is now a networking share-taker, not just a GPU vendor; its SpectrumX momentum pressures Arista and Cisco datacenter switch share.
insights.quote: Nvidia was the number one supplier of Ethernet switches in the datacenter, surpassing both Cisco and Arista Networks
insights.locator: The big news this time around

insights.claim: Nvidia's Q1 2026 datacenter Ethernet switch revenue was $2.1 billion, up 2.9X year on year, versus Arista's $2.07 billion (up 40.1 percent) and Cisco's $1.78 billion (up 43.2 percent), so Nvidia narrowly leads on far steeper growth.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Nvidia's ~3X growth rate versus ~40% for rivals implies continued share gains; watch Arista and Cisco datacenter switch guidance for erosion.
insights.quote: Nvidia's datacenter Ethernet switch revenues were $2.1 billion, up by a factor of 2.9X year on year
insights.locator: In Q1, Nvidia's datacenter Ethernet switch revenues

insights.claim: Nvidia's reported Ethernet switch figures cover SpectrumX switches only and exclude cables, NICs, DPUs, and InfiniBand gear, which contribute mightily to Nvidia's total switching revenue and understate its real networking footprint.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Nvidia's true networking revenue is materially larger than the $2.1B switch line; size the full networking TAM Nvidia is capturing, not just switches.
insights.quote: Nvidia's sales in this dataset are for SpectrumX Ethernet switches only, and does not include sales of cables, NICs, and DPUs
insights.locator: And by the way, we checked again with IDC

insights.claim: Nvidia ships only datacenter networking gear, whereas Arista, Cisco, and others also sell into edge and campus, so Nvidia's competitive threat is concentrated entirely in the high-growth datacenter segment.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Nvidia attacks the exact segment growing 61%, so incumbents' edge/campus revenue is not a defense where the money is moving.
insights.quote: Nvidia only ships datacenter gear, which Arista, Cisco, and others sell into edge and campus spaces.

themes.theme: 800G Transition and Collapsing Cost Per Bit

insights.claim: Sales of switches with 800 Gb/sec ports rose 10.3X to $3.58 billion in Q1 2026 and reached 35.8 percent of datacenter Ethernet sales, marking a rapid generational shift up the speed curve.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: 800G is the current volume inflection; suppliers with leading 800G switch/optics ramps capture the fastest-growing revenue pool.
insights.quote: Sales of switches with 800 Gb/sec ports rose by a very dramatic 10.3X to $3.58 billion in Q1 2026
insights.locator: Here is what is important as far as I am concerned.

insights.claim: 1.6 Tb/sec and even 3.2 Tb/sec switch ports are expected to appear soon, extending the port-speed roadmap beyond 800 Gb/sec.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The next capex wave is 1.6T/3.2T; track ASIC and optics vendors positioning for the post-800G cycle.
insights.quote: it won't be long now before we see 1.6 Tb/sec and even 3.2 Tb/sec ports appearing in switches
insights.locator: Here is what is important as far as I am concerned.

insights.claim: Native 200 Gb/sec and 400 Gb/sec switches fell to 34.1 percent of datacenter Ethernet sales at $3.41 billion, down 10.4 percent year on year and 52 percent sequentially, as buyers rapidly abandon the prior generation for 800G.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The 400G generation is rolling over fast; vendors overexposed to 200/400G face a sharp mix headwind.
insights.quote: switches with native 200 Gb/sec or 400 Gb/sec ports represented another 34.1 percent of the market, with revenues of $3.41 billion, off 10.4 percent year on year and off 52 percent sequentially
insights.locator: Switches with 800 Gb/sec ports (this is native

insights.claim: Port counts are estimated to have risen roughly 22X year on year, cutting cost per port and cost per bit by more than half, driven by both competitive pressure and technological advancement.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Falling cost per bit expands the addressable market and pressures pricing/margins even as revenue grows; model unit growth outpacing ASP.
insights.quote: the cost per port and therefore the cost per bit has been cut by more than half since a year ago. This is what happens when there is competitive pressure as well as technological advancement.
insights.locator: I think the port count has risen by a factor of 22X

insights.claim: A native 800 Gb/sec switch port costs about 61 cents per Gb/sec versus 53 cents for blended 200/400 Gb/sec, with 100 Gb/sec ports costing twice that and 1 Gb/sec ports $10-$11 per Gb/sec, so higher-speed ports deliver far cheaper bandwidth despite higher capital outlay.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The economics force large network builders up to 800G; low-speed ports survive only on low absolute capital cost, not efficiency.
insights.quote: It costs 61 cents per Gb/sec for a native 800 Gb/sec switch port, on average based on our estimates on port counts, compared to 53 cents per Gb/sec on a blended average of 200 Gb/sec and 400 Gb/sec ports.

insights.claim: For anyone building big and serious networks, 100 Gb/sec switches no longer suffice, leaving them viable only for enterprises stuck at 10 Gb/sec ports.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The serious-network floor has moved to 800G; demand for 100G switching is structurally capped to laggard enterprises.
insights.quote: except for enterprises stuck at 10 Gb/sec ports on their Ethernet networks, 100 Gb/sec switches just don't cut it anymore for anyone build big and serious networks
insights.locator: Clearly the shift is on to 800 Gb/sec gear
